title: I)E:\ ~:I,Ot'MENT,4l. ISIOI.O(:Y 96, 405-415 (1983) Quantitative Analysis of Cyclic AMP Waves Mediating Aggregation in Dictyostelium discoideum
text:
I)E:\ ~:I,Ot'MENT,4l. 
ISIOI.O(:Y 96, 405-415 (1983) 
Quantitative 
Analysis of Cyclic AMP Waves Mediating 
Aggregation 
in Dictyostelium 
discoideum 
PETER N. DEVREOTES,**’ MICHAEL J. PoTm,t 
ANI) STEPHEN A. MACKAY+ 
We have previously 
reported 
the detection 
of CAMP waves within 
monolayers 
of aggregatin~iUic.fyo,sfc~li/cr,, 
tli.scoidrrrr,r 
cells (K. J. Tomchik 
and P. N. Devreotes, 
1981, S’~ic,vrr, 212, 443-446). The computer-assisted 
analysis 
presented 
here 
of the fluorographic 
images of the CAMP waves reveals (1) all the waves have a consistent 
width and height; (21 CAMP 
concentrations 
within 
centers of concentric 
aggregation 
territories 
oscillate 
periodically 
while 
at spiral 
centers 
the 
concentration 
builds up to a plateau 
value within 
2 mm; (3) cells within 
the region of intersection 
of two oppositeI> 
directed 
crlMP 
waves are stimulated 
to produce more (AMP 
than those responding 
to a single wave; (4) cells start 
to move when the CAMP level begins to increase 
and cease movement 
when the peak c4MP 
concentration 
reaches 
the cell. 
INTRODIJCTION 
Dictyostelium 
discoideum grows as free-living 
amoe- 
bae until 
its nutrient 
supply is exhausted 
or removed. 
Within 
a few hours following 
starvation, 
monolayers 
of 
cells on an agar surface become segregated into terri- 
tories encompassing 
about 10” to 10” cells; cells within 
each territory 
are attracted 
to its center. The movement 
of the cells is periodic and highly 
organized. Time-lapse 
films reveal that individual 
cells move inwardly 
in steps, 
advancing 
for about 2 min and then stopping 
for about 
5 to 7 min before moving 
again. In dark-field 
photo- 
graphs, the coordinated 
movements 
can be visualized 
as white bands which form spiral or concentric 
circular 
patterns. 
The bands delineate 
the zones where, at that 
moment, cells are advancing chemotactically 
toujard the 
center. 
In time-lapse 
films, 
each band can be seen 
spreading outu!ardly 
from a center as an enlarging 
ring. 
The band maintains 
a constant width of about 100 cells, 
since as a cell at the distal edge begins its in/,uard move- 
ment step and elongates, one at the proximal 
edge ceases 
and becomes more rounded. The period for initiation 
of 
successive rings is about 5-7 min and each ring takes 
about 30 min to reach the edge of the territory. 
There is little 
doubt that in D. discoideuwz these or- 
ganized 
cell movements 
are directed 
by propagated 
waves of adenosine 3’:5’-monophosphate 
(CAMP). Cells 
are chemotactically 
attracted 
to CAMP, isolated 
cells 
secrete CAMP in response to CAMP stimuli, 
and a uni- 
form background 
level of the nonhydrolysable 
CAMP 
analogue, adenosine 3’:5’-cyclic phosphorothioate 
(CAMP- 
’ To whom all correspondence 
should be addressed. 
S), blocks aggregation 
(reviewed in Devreotes, 1982). We 
have previously 
reported 
an isotope-dilution 
fluorog- 
raphy technique 
for CAMP which allows direct visual- 
ization 
of the CAMP waves (Tomchik 
and Devreotes, 
1981). In this report we present a quantitative 
analysis 
of the fluorographic 
images of CAMP waves, focusing 
on the degree of variation 
in wave heights and widths, 
the CAMP concentration 
near the origin 
of the waves, 
the interaction 
of oppositely 
directed 
waves at their 
point of intersection, 
and the relative 
positions 
of mov- 
ing and signaling 
cells. Our results are consistent 
with 
biochemical 
measurements 
of CAMP levels in isolated 
cells and suggest a satisfactory 
explanation 
of cellular 
movements 
during 
aggregation. 
METHODS 
Cell culture 
and cell development. 
NC-4 cells were 
grown and allowed to develop patterns 
exactly 
as pre- 
viously 
described (Tomchik 
and Devreotes, 
1981). Ax-3 
cells were grown in HL-5 media to a density of 5 X lo6 
cells/ml. 
Cells were washed twice and resuspended 
in 
development 
buffer (5 mM NaeHP04, 5 mM KHZP04, 1 
mM MgS04, 200 PM CaC&.). Three milliliters 
(lo7 cells/ 
ml) was added to loo-mm starvation 
plates prepared as 
described for NC-4 cells. Patterns 
appeared in 3 to 4 hr 
at 22°C; the appearance was enhanced if the agar con- 
tained 1.5 mM caffeine. 
Isotope dilutionjuurography 
technique. The technique 
of isotope dilution-fluorography 
was carried out exactly 
as previously 
described (Tomchik 
and Devreotes, 
1981) 
except that 
47-mm filters 
were sometimes 
used and 
406 
DEVELOPMENTALBIOLOGY 
VOLUME 96,1983 
coated with 
a larger volume (8 ml/60 mm petri dish) 
of protein 
kinase. This resulted 
in a more uniform 
ad- 
sorption 
of kinase to the filter, 
More than 30 indepen- 
dent fluorographic 
images have been obtained 
by ap- 
plication 
of these techniques. 
The CAMP waves have 
been observed in aggregating 
monolayers 
of both NC- 
4 and Ax-3 strains 
of D. discoideum. 
These appear to 
be similar 
although 
those of strain Ax-3 have not been 
analyzed in detail. Previously 
published 
control 
exper- 
iments suggest that the bands of decreased optical den- 
sity on the fluorograph 
represent 
CAMP waves and are 
the morphogenic 
signals that guide cellular 
movements 
(Tomchik and Devreotes, 1981). A definitive 
control was 
provided by use of the drug caffeine which rapidly 
and 
specifically 
blocks CAMP-elicited 
CAMP production 
in 
isolated cells but has no effect on chemotaxis 
to external 
gradients 
(Brenner, 
personal communication; 
Theibert 
and Devreotes, submitted 
for publication). 
When cells 
are plated on gradients 
of caffeine, patterns 
of cell mo- 
tion are observed in the portion 
of the monolayer 
con- 
taining 
lower caffeine concentrations 
but disappear 
as 
the drug concentration 
reaches about 3 mM. In corre- 
sponding 
fluorographic 
images, the CAMP waves are 
visible 
in the region of low caffeine concentration 
but 
disappear 
at the same point as the organized 
cellular 
movements. In other control experiments, 
a small spot 
(~5 mm) of caffeine was applied to a cell monolayer 
just distal to an advancing 
band of elongated cells. As 
the band progressed through 
the caffeine-containing 
re- 
gion, a gap appeared in the band which 
closed as the 
band reached the opposite side of the drug-treated 
re- 
gion. Corresponding 
fluorographic 
images revealed that 
CAMP waves were absent from the drug-treated 
region. 
These experiments 
provide further 
evidence that prop- 
agated CAMP signals direct cellular 
motions. 
Quantitatim 
and resolution 
of the isotope-dilution 
jkorograph 
technique. All magnitudes 
are reported 
in 
relative 
units 
of optical 
density. 
It has not yet been 
possible to obtain detailed quantitative 
information 
on 
absolute CAMP concentrations. 
Estimates 
of CAMP con- 
centrations 
within 
waves were obtained 
from the fol- 
lowing considerations. 
Caffeine was applied to a small 
region (-5 mm diameter) 
of the monolayer 5 min before 
cellular CAMP was extracted. Although, 
as stated above, 
this treatment 
eliminated 
the bands of decreased op- 
tical density, there was little 
change in the optical den- 
sity of the valleys (i.e., regions between the CAMP-con- 
taining 
bands) within 
the caffeine-treated 
area com- 
pared with 
the adjacent 
control 
area. This indicates 
that the CAMP concentration 
in the valley 
regions is 
below the limit 
of detection 
(~5 X lo-’ M CAMP). The 
CAMP concentration 
at the peaks of the CAMP waves 
was estimated 
by applying 
standard 
concentrations 
in 
spots (1 to 2 mm) to regions devoid of waves immedi- 
ately (-5 see) before extraction 
of cellular 
CAMP. From 
the optical 
density 
in the spots appearing 
in the cor- 
responding 
fluorographic 
image the CAMP concentra- 
tion at the wave peaks was estimated to be greater than 
4 X lop7 M but less than 2 X 10m6 M. The short interval 
between application 
of the known concentration 
of CAMP 
and extraction 
assured that there was little degradation 
by phosphodiesterase 
and that cellular 
production 
of 
additional 
CAMP was minimal. 
These estimates 
indi- 
cate that CAMP concentrations 
change at least 20-fold 
from <5 X lop8 to about lo-” M CAMP. Although 
the 
changes in optical density were clearly a linear function 
of distance (see Results), those in CAMP concentration 
should be geometric 
due to the effect of the isotope- 
dilution 
detection 
technique. 
Therefore, 
changes 
in 
CAMP are expected to be greater 
than those reported 
in optical 
density. 
Nevertheless, 
all of the conclusions 
drawn from our analysis depend on relative 
rather than 
absolute changes in CAMP levels. 
In our protocol, the upper filter is washed 4 min after 
cellular 
CAMP is extracted 
from the monolayer. 
Little 
lateral convection 
of CAMP occurs between the vertical 
channels of the Millipore 
filter, but lateral 
diffusion 
is 
possible. The degree of diffusional 
distortion 
of the CAMP 
waves was estimated 
theoretically 
by considering 
a 
Guassian distribution 
of similar 
dimensions 
to the ob- 
served CAMP waves. The shape that distribution 
would 
have had 4 min earlier was estimated using the diffusion 
coefficient 
for CAMP in water. 
These estimates 
sug- 
gested that the CAMP waves at the time of extraction 
were lo-20% 
sharper than those appearing 
in the im- 
ages. Empirical 
comparison 
of diameters 
of standard 
CAMP spots with the diameters detected in correspond- 
ing fluorographs 
suggested a similar 
slight 
distortion 
(Tomchik 
and Devreotes, 1981). 
Microdensitometry 
of the fluorographic 
image. Fluo- 
rographs 
were digitized 
using an Optronics 
P-1000 ro- 
tating 
drum microdensitometer. 
Each scan produced a 
256 X 256 pixel matrix. 
By using a pixel size of either 
25, 50, or 100 pm a square section of the fluorograph 
6.4, 12.8, or 25.6 mm on a side, respectively, 
was digi- 
tized. Each pixel could take on gray levels between 0 
and 255, selected to correspond to a linear scale of either 
0 to 2 optical densities 
(OD) or 0 to 3 OD. Thus a pixel 
with an OD of 2 would be assigned 255 when the scanner 
was set at 2 OD full 
scale. The same pixel would be 
assigned 2/3 X 255 when the scanner was set at 3 OD 
full scale. In order to compare sections scanned at 2 OD 
with those scanned at 3 OD, the gray level of each pixel 
was adjusted 
so that 
the histogram 
of gray 
levels 
(-64,000 
pixels) of each scan had a mean of 120 and a 
standard 
deviation 
of 20. Digitized 
images were dis- 
played using a Digital 
Graphic 
Systems CAT-200 256 
X 256 X 8 bit image frame buffer and a Conrac SNA/ 
14C black-and-white 
video monitor. 
All image compu- 
tations were performed 
on a DEC PDP-11/40 
minicom- 
puter. 
In the images presented 
the gray scale is reversed 
from the original 
fluorograph 
(Figs. 1 and 4). The lowest 
values of optical density correspond to the highest CAMP 
concentrations 
and are displayed 
as the darkest parts 
of the images. In the figures, images have been low-pass 
filtered 
to reduce the graininess 
present in the Auoro- 
graph 
and are displayed 
with 
an artificially 
reduced 
number 
of gray levels to achieve a contoured 
effect. 
However, all computations 
(e.g., cross sections and wave 
averages) described were not manipulated 
in this way. 
Estimation 
of elevated CAMP within 
“‘unnihilation 
xor~es. ” Statistics 
were produced from the four lowest 
magnification 
scans (100 pm/pixel) 
(including 
Figs. lA, 
B), each one representing 
a different 
2.56 X 2.56-cm area 
of the fluorograph. 
By inspecting 
the images on the video 
display, annihilation 
zones (i.e., areas where it was ob- 
vious from the geometry of the patterns 
that there were 
intersections 
of the CAMP-containing 
bands) were se- 
lected. Two of us scored each zone as positive 
if it con- 
tained a more elevated CAMP concentration 
than the 
parts of the bands outside the zone. 
More quantitative 
statistics 
were obtained 
by study- 
ing the gray level distributions 
of the four images. First, 
artifactual 
high-intensity 
areas caused by trapping 
of 
air bubbles between 
the two filters 
(see Fig. 1) were 
eliminated 
from consideration 
by using an “area mark- 
ing” program 
to draw over the air bubbles with a gray 
level of 255, which otherwise 
never appears in the im- 
ages. The various gray level frequency 
distribution 
pro- 
grams ignored any pixels with gray level 255 and used 
all the remaining 
pixels 
to compute 
a frequency 
dis- 
tribution. 
Next, the same area marking 
program 
was used to 
draw over all the annihilation 
zones with 
a gray level 
of 255 and then a second frequency 
distribution 
was 
computed for the image minus these zones (and minus 
the air bubbles). The “percentage 
of total area in an- 
nihilation 
zones” was computed as the number of pixels 
in the annihilation 
zones divided 
by the total number 
of pixels in the images. All parts of the bands adjacent 
to the zone in question were excluded, not just the high- 
intensity 
zone narrowly 
defined, so that the percentage 
of total 
area in annihilation 
zones is not underesti- 
mated. 
An overall 
threshold 
gray value that 
best defined 
higher 
than average CAMP concentrations 
was deter- 
mined by eye without 
looking 
at the numbers. 
Agree- 
ment on a threshold 
value of <94 was obtained 
as des- 
ignating 
“high-intensity” 
pixels. Similarly, 
a threshold 
of ~118 was determined 
as the typical 
threshold 
of the 
CAMP-containing 
bands. (The “band” 
perceived by eye 
corresponds 
roughly 
to the width 
of the peak at half- 
height.) 
From 
the total 
image distribution, 
the per- 
centage of total area at high intensity 
was computed 
as the percentage 
of pixels in the entire 
image lower 
than the 94 high-intensity 
threshold. 
Similarly, 
the per- 
centage of total 
area in bands was computed 
as the 
percentage of pixels in the entire image lower than the 
118 threshold. 
From the gray level distributions 
of the images with 
the annihilation 
zones removed, 
and the total 
image 
distribution, 
the distribution 
of pixels 
in the annihi- 
lation 
zones could be determined. 
The percentage 
of 
high-intensity 
pixels in annihilation 
zones was thus the 
number of high-intensity 
pixels that were in these zones 
divided 
by the number 
of high-intensity 
pixels in the 
total 
image. Similarly, 
the percentage 
of the annihi- 
lation 
zone area at high intensity 
was computed. 
By 
considering 
only the pixels less than 118 (=wave area) 
and comparing 
to the distribution 
for the annihilation 
zones only, an estimate of percentage of the total band 
area in annihilation 
zones was obtained. 
A ~erugr cross sections of CAMP WULWS. To examine 
overall 
shape of waves, average cross sections 
were 
measured 
in the highest 
magnification 
(25 pm/pixel) 
images. Since the waves are not completely 
uniform 
(see 
Results) a single cross section might 
be misleading 
if 
it crossed through 
an intense or weak area. An average 
cross section of an entire isolated wave (or pair of suc- 
cessive waves if possible) 
was desired. First, 
a curve 
drawn 
along the wave ridge was digitized. 
The curve 
usually 
covered a distance 
of 5-6 mm along the ridge 
of the wave. Then the normal vector every 25 pm along 
this curve was determined 
and a cross section of gray 
levels was taken along each normal vector. Finally, 
the 
set of cross sections was averaged to produce the plot 
of average gray level versus distance 
from the wave 
ridge. 
All wave averages were computed using unsmoothed 
data. The wave averages were done in two widths, 
75 
pixels (1875 pm) and 150 pixels (3750 Fm). All of the 
‘75-wide averages were centered on wave ridges. All of 
the 150-wide averages were centered on valleys (so that 
with 
150 wide they show two waves). On 7 different 
scans, 24 average cross sections were done, and the sum- 
mary data are in Table 1. 
Optical density determinutio~~ along the> crest of an or- 
igin qf (x spiral /o(Lzje. A technique 
similar 
to the average 
cross section was used to determine 
the average gray 
level along the ridge of a wave. Again, a curve defining 
the wave ridge was specified, and normal vectors to the 
wave ridge were computed at 25-pm intervals. 
Instead 
of a cross section of gray level along each normal vector, 
the gray levels of 15 pixels along the normal 
(seven on 
each side of the ridge) were averaged. This value gave

order to compare the features in the dark-field 
and fluo-

image. Be- 
rograph 
images, it was necessary to align the two im-

of the wave patterns,

a common scale, a number

of common features were measured in the two images 
using a magnifying 
reticle. These features 
included 
an 
entire filter diameter 
and the distances between widely 
RESULTS 
spaced aggregation 
centers. A scaling 
factor 
relating 
All of the analyses of CAMP waves presented 
here 
the two images was thus determined 
(standard 
error 
were taken from our best fluorographic 
image which 
DEVREOTES, 
POTEL, 
AND 
MACKAY 
Quantitaticm of CAMP Waves iv1 Dictyostelium 
has been previously 
published 
(Tomchik 
and Devreotes, 
1981). Figure 
1 shows a series of images taken from 
different 
regions of the fluorograph. 
Perusal 
of these 
images reveals many of the features 
which 
are docu- 
mented in this report: 
(1) The images consist of dark 
bands (about 0.8 mm wide) separated 
by wider 
light 
regions (about 1.2 mm). (2) The widths of the dark bands 
are relatively 
constant 
in the majority 
of the cases, 
although 
there are a few bands which 
appear much 
thinner 
(particularly 
the innermost 
in spiral patterns). 
(3) Typically 
the areas at which two bands meet (des- 
ignated 
annihilation 
zones) are darker 
than 
the re- 
mainder 
of the band (i.e., contain 
higher 
than average 
CAMP concentrations). 
(4) There 
are darker 
patches 
(about 0.5 to 1.5 mm in diameter) 
at more or less random 
intervals 
along the dark bands (designated 
“hot spots”). 
In contrast, 
the valleys (light 
regions between the dark 
bands) are relatively 
uniform 
even within 
large areas 
devoid of bands. 
Dimensions 
of the CAMP Waves 
To determine the dimensions of the CAMP waves, cross 
sections of the dark bands were analyzed. As described 
under Methods the above-mentioned 
variations 
in den- 
sity 
(i.e., hot spots), if present 
in any given 
region 
scanned, were averaged out. Figure 2 illustrates 
a typ- 
ical cross section. The majority 
of the peaks were sym- 
metric (i.e., the magnitude 
of the slopes on the leading 
and trailing 
edges were similar). 
There was little 
evi- 
dence that the waves or the measurement 
technique 
saturated 
since none of the profiles had flattened 
tops 
(also see Fig. 4). 
The dimensions 
of the CAMP waves were computed 
410

the cross section. High CAMP 
concentrations

to “up” in graph. Each point corresponds

bars show standard 
error 
along wave. Aggregation 
center is to left. 
from 24 cross sections taken from independent 
regions 
of the monolayer 
(9 cm diameter). 
It was noteworthy 
that the majority 
of the bands had very similar 
profiles. 
The widths of the peaks at half-height 
ranged from 500 
to 900 pm and averaged about 700 pm. The intensity 
of 
the majority 
of the peaks appearing in the fluorographic 
image were similar 
and correspond 
to a CAMP concen- 
tration 
of about 4 X 10m7 to 2 X lo-’ M. The optical den- 
sity of the valleys on the proximal 
and distal edges were 
also equal and corresponded 
to a CAMP concentration 
of ~5 x 10 * M (Fig. 2, Table 1, see Methods). 
Peak-to-peak 
distance 
was measured 
in six cases 
where cross sections passed through 
consecutive 
peaks 
(i.e., similar 
to those shown in Fig. 2). These values 
ranged from 1400 to 1750 pm with an average of about 
1600 pm. Thus, the width of the peak at half-maximum 
(-700 
grn) is about 43% of the measured wavelength. 
The percentage of total area in bands calculated 
from 
threshold 
pixel 
values 
as described 
under 
Methods 
was 46.670. 
The great majority 
of the CAMP waves detected in 
the fluorograph 
image had the characteristics 
outlined 
above (but 
see later). 
This 
quantitative 
assessment 
agrees with the impression 
derived from visual inspec- 
tion of the fluorographic 
image (Fig. 1). The width 
of 
the peak at half-maximum 
is perceived as a band when 
the fluorograph 
is examined by eye. For the fluorograph 
reported 
on here the widths 
of the bands measured 
manually 
were about 0.7 to 0.8 mm. In seven indepen- 
dent fluorographic 
images the measured widths 
were 
similar 
(from 0.7 to 0.9 mm). 
AnrGhilation 
Zones between Oppositely Directed 
Waves 
As noted earlier the intensity 
of the image within 
the 
region where two oppositely 
directed CAMP-containing 
bands met and were annihilated 
often appeared to be 
greater 
(i.e., have higher 
CAMP concentrations) 
than 
the remaining 
regions of the bands (Fig. lE, F, G, H). 
This is clearly evident in a pentagonal 
pattern resulting 
from the collision 
of five bands shown in Fig. 1F. Note 
the elevated CAMP level (i.e., decreased optical density) 
at each of the vertices where the bands join. The in- 
creased intensity 
within 
these annihilation 
zones sug- 
gests an interaction 
occurs as the waves meet. 
We sought to document these observations. 
This was 
first done by visual inspection 
of many images such as 
those presented 
in Fig. 1 (see Methods). 
By this test 
74.2% of the annihilation 
zones scored as positive. 
Sta- 
tistical 
analysis bore out the visual test. While the an- 
nihilation 
zones constituted 
only 11.4% of the total im- 
age area and 20.2% of the total area occupied by bands, 
42.8% of our designated 
high-intensity 
pixels were lo- 
cated within 
these zones. Furthermore, 
while only 5.8% 
of the total 
image and 12.4% of the total band area 
consisted 
of high-intensity 
pixels, 
21.6% of the total 
annihilation 
zone area was comprised of high-intensity 
pixels. Although 
not analyzed quantitatively, 
it was ap- 
parent 
from visual 
inspection 
of independent 
fluoro- 
graphic images that higher CAMP concentrations 
within 
the annihilation 
zones is a general feature of the CAMP 
waves. 
It was also noted that random 
patches within 
the 
CAMP-containing 
bands had higher than average image 
intensities 
comparable 
to those found in the majority 
of the annihilation 
zones. Such hot spots never appeared 
in the valley regions between the bands. These hot spots 
are included 
in the statistical 
analysis 
presented 
pre- 
viously. However, the fraction 
of annihilation 
zones with 
high intensities 
is much greater than is expected if the 
hot spots appeared at random in the image or within 
the bands. The hot spots could not be attributed 
to dif- 
ferences in cell density 
which 
was quite uniform 
and 
would not, in any event, be related to the geometry 
of 
the patterns. 
Origin 
of CAMP Waves ut Aggregation 
Centers 
The features 
of the CAMP waves in the central 
re- 
gions of the aggregation 
territories 
were examined. 
While the great majority 
of territories 
had spiral cen- 
ters, particularly 
in counterrotating 
pairs, a few of the 
territories 
were characterized 
by concentric 
“bullseye” 
patterns. 
Some of these had a central 
“dot” 
of low op- 
tical density (high CAMP) surrounded 
by a ring (latest 
CAMP wave to have left the center). In others, the ring 
was smaller 
and the central 
dot was absent. In four

concentrations 
detected in the fluorographic 
image cor- 
responded to regions of elongated cells in the cell mono-

along a spiral wave. Plot shows

corresponds

layer (white bands in dark-field 
photographs). 
This re- 
sult was documented with greater precision by aligning 
the fluorograph 
and dark-field 
photograph 
computa- 
tionally 
(see Methods). The distal (from the center) edge 
of the light 
band in the dark-field 
image is delineated 
by a black line which was used as a marker for align- 
ment. As shown in Figs. 4A-C the black line aligns on 
the distal 
edge of the CAMP wave. Notice that since 
waves are moving 
in every direction 
in this 
image, 
alignment 
with the leading edge in all parts of the im- 
age is the only possibility, 
given that the images were 
scaled and rotated reliably. 
The alignment 
is illustrated 
graphically 
by comparison 
of optical density cross sec- 
tions 
through 
the dark-field 
photograph 
of the cell 
monolayer 
and fluorographic 
image of the CAMP waves 
(Fig. 4D). The point at which the cells start to elongate, 
indicate by the black line in the dark-field 
image, occurs 
just proximal 
to the point at which the CAMP concen- 
tration 
represented 
on the fluorographic 
image starts 
to increase. 
DISCUSSION 
The fluorographic 
images demonstrate 
that 
CAMP 
waves indeed propagate 
through 
the aggregating 
cell 
monolayer. 
The quantitation 
presented 
in this report 
gives detailed 
information 
on the dimensions 
and rel- 
ative intensities 
of these waves. Several conclusions 
can 
be derived from our analysis: 
(1) The dimensions and intensities 
of the CAMP waves 
were remarkably 
consistent 
for those taken from many 
independent 
regions within 
a g-cm-diameter 
field. This 
consistency 
allows a typical 
CAMP wave to be defined. 
The width 
of the peak at half-maximum 
is about 700 
pm and occupies about 43% (700 pm/1600 pm) of the 
peak-to-peak 
distance. 
(2) Annihilation 
zones, the regions in which two op- 
positely directed CAMP waves meet and extinguish, 
con- 
tain more CAMP than found on average along the CAMP- 
containing 
bands. However, 
it was puzzling 
that small 
patches appearing 
at random along the bands were also 
of higher 
intensity. 
(3) Evidence suggests that CAMP levels at the centers 
of the concentric patterns oscillate with a period of about 
5-7 min. The intensity 
of the bands near spiral centers 
frequently 
started 
at very low levels and increased 
to 
a plateau over a distance 
of about 2 mm. In counter- 
rotating 
spirals, the two origins joined to form a longer 
stretch of low intensity. 
(4) Overlays of the dark-field 
photograph 
of the cell 
monolayer 
and the fluorographic 
image of the CAMP 
waves which 
were carried 
out by computer 
analysis 
qualitatively 
agreed with 
those done by visual 
inspec- 
tion. These suggest that the regions of elongated 
cells 
correspond 
to those with elevated CAMP. 
In every fluorographic 
image examined 
the patterns 
of CAMP-containing 
bands matched identically 
the pat- 
terns of light bands on a dark background 
seen in dark- 
field photographs 
of the aggregating 
cell monolayers. 
Clearly the outward velocity of the regions of cell shape 
change is equal to the velocity 
of propagation 
of the 
CAMP waves. For the fluorograph 
analyzed 
in this re- 
port, that velocity 
was about 300 pm/min. 
Although 
it 
is appreciated 
that this velocity 
may vary for different 
waves in the field (Gross et al., 1976), the average value 
can be used to convert all radial 
distances to approxi- 
mate times. The conversion 
is simply: time = distance/ 
velocity. 
The width 
of the peak at half-height 
of 700 
pm, when corrected for diffusional 
spreading, 
suggests 
that the major part of the relayed CAMP signal lasts 
for 2.0 min (Table 1). The period between 
successive 
signals is about 5.5 min (no correction 
for diffusion 
is 
necessary here). These values are remarkably 
consis- 
tent with biochemical 
measurements 
of the time course 
of elevated CAMP levels in synchronized 
cell suspen- 
sions (Gerish and Wick, 1975; Shaffer, 1975; Roos et al., 
1975; Gerish et al., 1977; Geller and Brenner, 
1978; De- 
vreotes and Steck, 1979; Dinauer 
et al., 1980a-c). 
It has been shown that the cells elongate close to the 
time that the leading edge of the peak reaches the po- 
sition 
of the cell (Tomchik 
and Devreotes, 
1981) (Fig. 
4). Note in Fig. 4 that the distances between the black

fluorography 
technique 
measures total CAMP 
extracted 
from the cell monolayer 
(i.e., intracellular 
plus extracellular 
cAMP). In experiments 
with cell suspensions, 
the extracellular 
cAMP 
level reaches a maximum 
about 15 set later 
than the intracellular 
level (Gerisch 
and Wick, 1975; Dinauer et al., 1980a). Given that sim- 
ilar conditions 
for degradation 
of extracellular 
CAMP exist in plate 
and suspension 
cultures, 
the extracellular 
CAMP wave is envisioned

of a movement 
step, 100 see, measured 
from

films

ceases as 
the direction 
of the gradient 
reverses. However, 
final 
interpretation 
should 
be reserved 
until 
simultaneous 
measurements 
of movement 
step durations 
and CAMP 
waves are made.

indicates 
that the interval 
between 
the black line and the crest of the extra- 
cellular 
CAMP wave is 85 to 105 sec. 
414 
DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY 
VOLUME 96, 1983 
In the dark-field 
photograph, 
the width 
of the white 
bands, 
which 
correspond 
to elongated 
cells (Alcantara 
and Monk, 19’74), appeared 
to span nearly 
the full width 
of the CAMP wave peaks (Fig. 4). This may suggest 
that 
the cells remain 
elongated 
for about 
a minute 
after 
the 
wave 
crest 
has 
passed 
and 
directed 
movement 
has 
ceased. 
However, 
we have 
noted 
that 
the 
apparent 
widths 
of the white 
bands 
varies 
widely 
in different 
cultures 
and in the same culture 
at different 
times 
and 
may depend 
critically 
on the fluid layer 
above the cells, 
the 
temperature, 
and 
strain 
of Dictyostelium. 
It 
is 
therefore 
doubtful 
that 
conclusions 
on movement 
step 
durations 
can be drawn 
from 
the widths 
of the white 
bands. 
Roos and Newell 
(1981) have isolated 
“streamer” 
mutants 
in which nearly 
the entire 
wavelength 
consists 
of a white 
band. 
These 
mutants 
may remain 
elongated 
for several 
minutes 
after 
the wave crest has passed 
and 
movement 
has ceased. 
Our statistical 
analysis 
indicates 
that 
higher 
CAMP 
concentrations 
are found within 
the annihilation 
zones, 
the regions 
where 
two oppositely 
directed 
CAMP waves 
meet. 
Since 
the concentration 
of extracellular 
CAMP 
attenuates 
dramatically 
with diffusion 
over a short 
dis- 
tance, 
the higher 
intensity 
in the annihilation 
zones 
likely represents 
a stronger 
response 
of the cells in that 
region. 
This finding 
indicates 
that cells within 
the nor- 
mal wave peak are not responding 
maximally. 
A deli- 
cate balance 
between 
secretion 
rate 
and phosphodies- 
terase 
activity 
may keep the stimulus 
below a maximum 
level. Our estimates 
of total CAMP concentrations 
within 
the peaks of the waves (10m6 M) are consistent 
with this 
assumption. 
(The cellular 
response 
saturates 
at an ex- 
tracellular 
stimulus 
concentration 
of lo-” M CAMP.) 
As outlined 
previously, 
examination 
of concentric 
ter- 
ritories 
suggests 
that 
CAMP concentrations 
within 
the 
central 
cells oscillate 
periodically. 
Since 
the signaling 
time 
is about 
a third 
of the oscillation 
period 
(2 min/ 
6 min), 
concentric 
territories 
should 
have 
detectable 
CAMP at their 
centers 
in about 
a third 
of the cases. Of 
12 concentric 
centers 
examined, 
4 had elevated 
CAMP 
in the central 
region. 
These observations 
are consistent 
with those of spontaneous 
CAMP oscillations 
in shaken 
cell suspensions 
(Gerisch 
and Hess, 
1974) and suggest 
that 
centers 
consist 
of a cell or group 
of cells 
which 
oscillate 
spontaneously. 
However, 
the resolution 
of our 
technique 
is not sufficiently 
high to rule out that 
a few 
central 
cells continuously 
release 
CAMP (which 
we can- 
not 
detect) 
and 
trigger 
the 
nearby 
cells 
to oscillate 
(which 
we do detect). 
This seems 
unlikely 
based 
on the 
known 
properties 
of the response 
where 
continuous 
ap- 
plication 
of CAMP causes 
adaptation 
after 
several 
min- 
utes. 
It is more 
likely 
that 
a group 
of central 
cells os- 
cillate 
according 
to a mechanism 
based 
on the known 
properties 
of the response-adaptation 
system 
(Dinauer 
et al., 1980b). Mathematical 
models 
have 
been 
devised 
which account 
for both spontaneous 
oscillations 
and re- 
lay of CAMP signals 
through 
the monolayer 
(Goldbeter 
and Segel, 1977). Similar 
mathematical 
models 
also lead 
to spiral 
wave solutions 
in which 
the CAMP concentra- 
tion builds 
up over a distance 
of about 
one wavelength 
away from the tip of the spiral 
(Hagan 
and Cohen, 1981). 
These calculations 
are consistent 
with our measure- 
ments of CAMP concentrations 
which 
build 
up to a pla- 
teau within 
a few millimeters 
from the tip of the spiral. 
We have 
previously 
proposed 
that 
the leading 
edge 
of the wave peak provides 
the gradient 
which 
attracts 
cells toward 
the aggregation 
center 
(Tomchik 
and De- 
vreotes, 
1981). The radial 
distance 
spanned 
by the gra- 
dient 
is a function 
of the velocity 
of wave propagation 
and the kinetics 
of CAMP secretion. 
The measured 
wave 
velocity 
is approximately 
300 pm/min 
and the rate 
of 
CAMP secretion 
measured 
in isolated 
cells 
reaches 
a 
maximum 
after about 2.0 min. This results 
in a gradient 
that spans 
a radial 
distance 
of about 
700 pm. In theory, 
the same gradients 
could be formed 
only by alteration 
of both parameters 
simultaneously. 
For instance, 
were 
the wave velocity 
faster 
(or slower) 
but the kinetics 
of 
CAMP secretion 
also faster 
(or slower), 
the same 
gra- 
dient would be set up. Apparently, 
through 
evolutionary 
adaptation, 
the gradient-sensing 
mechanism 
has been 
closely 
integrated 
with 
both the kinetics 
of the signal- 
ing response 
and 
the wave 
velocity 
such 
that 
aggre- 
gation 
will be successful 
over a wide range 
of environ- 
mental 
conditions. 
The cell-to-cell 
relay 
of a chemical 
signal 
is a simple 
mechanism 
for a group 
of identical 
cells to communi- 
cate. All the cells in the monolayer 
have the same 
re- 
sponse 
(i.e., activation 
and 
subsequent 
adaptation 
of 
adenylate 
cyclase) 
to extracellular 
CAMP. Yet this 
is 
sufficient 
to spontaneously 
produce 
uniform 
chemical 
waves which 
propagate 
through 
the monolayer 
and or- 
ganize 
cellular 
aggregation. 
It is possible 
that 
similar 
mechanisms 
operate 
in other systems 
where form arises 
in a population 
of identical 
cells.
